_comment: HAND-AUTHORED adjudications, merged over the extracted hypothesis by scripts/build-capability-manifest.mjs. This file is the source of truth and is never generated; capabilities.json IS generated and must never be hand-edited. Each entry records what a human decided about one (op|enum, dialect) cell: `kind` native|absent, `name` the engine's OWN term for it, and `refusal` the exact message when absent. Filling all four dialects with the SAME name is what makes a capability shared — sharedness is DERIVED from the names agreeing, never authored. Leave `name` empty when the kind is settled but the surface term is still a design decision; the cell then stays unadjudicated on purpose.

top._why: T-SQL has TOP and, since 2012, OFFSET/FETCH. They are two different constructs, not two spellings of one — SQL Server gained OFFSET/FETCH later for standards compliance and kept TOP. So MSSQL genuinely has both, and the other three have only the LIMIT concept, which is reached by limit(). Through 10.x .top(n) was SILENTLY DISCARDED on the other three: the row cap the caller wrote vanished with no error and no clause. Fixed 2026-07-20.

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no TOP clause — use limit() instead

postgres.refusal: Postgres has no TOP clause — use limit() instead

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no TOP clause — use limit() instead

limitWithTies._why: WITH TIES is real in MSSQL and Postgres and absent from MySQL and SQLite. The two that have it spell it differently — T-SQL `TOP (n) WITH TIES`, Postgres `FETCH FIRST n ROWS WITH TIES` — but both engines use the phrase WITH TIES, so the shared term is real rather than coined. Through 10.x MSSQL emitted `OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT n ROWS WITH TIES`, which no T-SQL production allows: <offset_fetch> ends in a mandatory ONLY and TOP admits no offset. Fixed 2026-07-20 to render through TOP. NAME LEFT EMPTY DELIBERATELY: `limitWithTies` is SQLEasy's coinage, not either engine's term. The honest term is `withTies`, but renaming is an API decision that belongs with the typed per-engine surface, not with this behavioural fix.

mysql.refusal: MySQL does not support WITH TIES

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support WITH TIES

joinOuterApply._why:
MSSQL OUTER APPLY is native and takes no ON clause. Postgres and MySQL express the same semantics as LEFT JOIN LATERAL, which is equally native — but a LEFT JOIN REQUIRES ON or USING, and an APPLY has no ON conditions by construction, so the emitted statement had no ON at all and neither engine could parse it. Closed with the documented `ON TRUE` idiom 2026-07-20. SQLite has neither LATERAL nor APPLY.

NAMES LEFT EMPTY DELIBERATELY, and this op is the design's own worked example: `outerApply` is T-SQL vocabulary, yet the capability is genuinely native on three engines. Because all three cells read `native`, nothing about a per-engine type split would move it off the shared surface — a Postgres user would still hit the dot and see a T-SQL term. Only derived sharedness forces the question, by requiring someone to assert `outerApply` is a real term in Postgres and MySQL, which it is not. The rename belongs with the typed surface. See docs/capability-manifest-design.md.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support OUTER APPLY/LATERAL joins

forUpdate._why:
Postgres and MySQL have a statement-level FOR UPDATE that locks every table the statement reads — one clause, no placement question. MSSQL has no such clause at all: its nearest equivalent is a WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK) hint, and T-SQL's table_hint production attaches to a table_or_view_name, i.e. ONE table reference. Through 10.x the hint was emitted only in the FROM-table branch, so a joined table was read at plain READ COMMITTED while the caller believed the whole result was locked, and a derived-table or TVF source got no hint anywhere. Silent, and a data-integrity bug rather than a syntax one.

The corpus could not have caught it: of 322 cases, 8 use a row lock and NONE combines one with a join or a non-table FROM. A hand-written test asserted the bug outright, named 'MSSQL applies the hint to the FROM table even alongside a JOIN'.

Fixed 2026-07-20: the hint now reaches every table reference, and MSSQL REFUSES where T-SQL has nowhere to put one. SQLite has no row-level locking at any version — its locking is whole-database/WAL-level.

MSSQL NAME = 'updlock' (Sandy's decision 2026-07-20). forUpdate is Postgres/MySQL vocabulary; a T-SQL DBA reaches for UPDLOCK, which is exactly what the hint emits — WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK). So the shared portable name does NOT derive here: Postgres and MySQL name it forUpdate, MSSQL names it updlock, and those are different real terms. The METHOD rename to .updlock() on the MSSQL builder lands with the typed surface; this cell records the target name now so the surface work has it.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

joinCrossApply._why: Same shape as joinOuterApply and unaffected by its bug: CROSS APPLY translates to CROSS JOIN LATERAL, and a CROSS JOIN never accepts an ON clause, so nothing was missing. Native on MSSQL, Postgres and MySQL; SQLite has neither. Names left empty for the same vocabulary reason as joinOuterApply.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support CROSS APPLY/LATERAL joins

onConflictDoUpdate._why:
Postgres (9.5+) and SQLite (3.24+) have a real conflict TARGET: ON CONFLICT (cols) DO UPDATE. MySQL does not — ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE fires on ANY unique or primary key and its grammar accepts no column list. Through 10.x naming columns on MySQL was silently discarded, so 'conflict on email' became 'conflict on any unique key', which matches a DIFFERENT set of rows and can update rows the caller never intended. Fixed 2026-07-20: MySQL refuses a named target and still accepts the untargeted form, which is honest about what the engine means. MSSQL is NOT adjudicated here — it has no upsert primitive at all and is currently synthesized as MERGE without HOLDLOCK; that is queued for re-homing under an explicit merge surface.

MSSQL ADJUDICATED 2026-07-20: T-SQL has NO upsert primitive. This used to abandon the INSERT grammar and synthesize a MERGE — a different statement with different atomicity, trigger and error semantics, which the caller never wrote and could not see. It was unsafe as written too: an un-hinted MERGE used as an upsert is race-prone at READ COMMITTED and can raise a duplicate-key violation, which ON CONFLICT never does. The repo already knew this (forShare emitted WITH (HOLDLOCK)), so the omission was an oversight. MERGE is genuine native T-SQL and should return as an EXPLICIT surface named in T-SQL's own vocabulary — that belongs with the per-engine typed builders, because naming it now would mint `mergeMssql()`, the suffixed form Sandy rejected.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

onConflictDoNothing._why:
Same conflict-target story as onConflictDoUpdate. Postgres/SQLite spell it DO NOTHING with an optional target; MySQL spells it INSERT IGNORE, which is a genuinely different construct — it suppresses OTHER errors too, not just duplicate keys — and accepts no target. Both engines' terms are real, and they are DIFFERENT terms, so this does not derive shared. MSSQL unadjudicated pending the merge re-home.

MSSQL ADJUDICATED 2026-07-20: T-SQL has NO upsert primitive. This used to abandon the INSERT grammar and synthesize a MERGE — a different statement with different atomicity, trigger and error semantics, which the caller never wrote and could not see. It was unsafe as written too: an un-hinted MERGE used as an upsert is race-prone at READ COMMITTED and can raise a duplicate-key violation, which ON CONFLICT never does. The repo already knew this (forShare emitted WITH (HOLDLOCK)), so the omission was an oversight. MERGE is genuine native T-SQL and should return as an EXPLICIT surface named in T-SQL's own vocabulary — that belongs with the per-engine typed builders, because naming it now would mint `mergeMssql()`, the suffixed form Sandy rejected.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

onConflictDoUpdateRaw._why:
Raw-SET variant of onConflictDoUpdate; same conflict-target and MSSQL story.

MSSQL ADJUDICATED 2026-07-20: T-SQL has NO upsert primitive. This used to abandon the INSERT grammar and synthesize a MERGE — a different statement with different atomicity, trigger and error semantics, which the caller never wrote and could not see. It was unsafe as written too: an un-hinted MERGE used as an upsert is race-prone at READ COMMITTED and can raise a duplicate-key violation, which ON CONFLICT never does. The repo already knew this (forShare emitted WITH (HOLDLOCK)), so the omission was an oversight. MERGE is genuine native T-SQL and should return as an EXPLICIT surface named in T-SQL's own vocabulary — that belongs with the per-engine typed builders, because naming it now would mint `mergeMssql()`, the suffixed form Sandy rejected.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

forShare._why:
Postgres and MySQL 8.0+ have a statement-level FOR SHARE. MSSQL does NOT, and the WITH (HOLDLOCK, ROWLOCK) it used to emit was not a near-equivalent: HOLDLOCK is a SYNONYM FOR SERIALIZABLE and takes key-range locks that prevent phantom inserts, which FOR SHARE does not do. Emitting it silently escalated the caller's isolation level. T-SQL's hint taxonomy splits into lock hints (UPDLOCK/XLOCK/ROWLOCK) and isolation hints (HOLDLOCK/REPEATABLEREAD/SERIALIZABLE), and there is no shared-mode counterpart to UPDLOCK in the first group.

REPEATABLEREAD was considered and rejected: it is still a read-isolation instruction that happens to leave shared locks behind rather than a lock REQUEST, and it changes how every other row the statement scans is read. Under 'no approximations' that is a synthesis.

Refused on MSSQL 2026-07-20. It should return under T-SQL's own vocabulary with the per-engine typed builders, where a caller naming HOLDLOCK or REPEATABLEREAD gets exactly what they asked for. forUpdate is unaffected — UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK is Microsoft's own documented idiom for it. SQLite has no row-level locking at any version.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no shared row lock — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE; use forUpdate() or take the isolation level explicitly

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

merge._why: MERGE is native T-SQL and exists on no other dialect. RE-HOMED 2026-07-20 as an explicit surface — QueryBuilder.merge((m) => …) over a MergeBuilder — after Sandy approved bringing it back. It expresses MERGE as T-SQL defines it (INTO/USING/ON/WHEN MATCHED/WHEN NOT MATCHED [BY SOURCE]/OUTPUT/mandatory semicolon/WITH (HOLDLOCK)), NOT the merge(conflictColumns, updates) upsert-keyhole a reviewer flagged as a worse lie than mergeMssql(). Through 7015f6c it had been REFUSED, because the only MERGE in the codebase was the silent INSERT-becomes-MERGE upsert substitution, which was removed. This is its honest return. HOLDLOCK is exposed via .holdlock(), documented as the concurrency-safe form, and NOT injected — the builder emits the MERGE the caller wrote. Name = 'merge' with no suffix: it collides with nothing, so when the per-engine typed builders land it moves verbatim to MssqlQueryBuilder and the runtime refusal on the other three becomes a compile-time absence.

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

postgres.refusal: Postgres has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

into._why: MERGE is native T-SQL and exists nowhere else here, so every part of its grammar is MSSQL-only — not just the merge() entry point but each sub-builder method the MergeBuilder exposes (builder/merge.ts). The other three refuse with 'Merge: <Dialect> has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only', verified by running each one. Recorded because the manifest was shipping these as native + adjudicated on all four: presence on a typed view is not performance, and these methods live on a sub-builder the view never gated. SQLEasy 10 synthesized a MERGE on MSSQL for upsert and it was race-prone without HOLDLOCK, which is exactly why holdlock() is part of this grammar rather than implied.

mysql.refusal: Merge: MySQL has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

postgres.refusal: Merge: Postgres has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

sqlite.refusal: Merge: SQLite has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

fromTableWithOwner._why: Owner (schema) qualification. In MySQL the 'schema' IS the database the connection already targets, so there is no second namespace to qualify with and the parser refuses a NON-EMPTY owner: '<Area>: MySQL does not support table owners'. The method deliberately stays ON the MySQL view (typed-views.ts) because an EMPTY owner is legal there — a conditional refusal, not a hidden method. That is precisely what made the manifest wrong: presence was being read as native, so it asserted MySQL performs owner qualification while the parser threw. The CAPABILITY is absent on MySQL; the method merely remains callable in its degenerate form.

mysql.refusal: From: MySQL does not support table owners

mysql.refusal: Update: MySQL does not support table owners

CallKind.Function._why: default-call.ts emits a function as a SELECT expression: Postgres and MSSQL emit 'SELECT ' (scalar) or 'SELECT * FROM ' (result set) at lines 95-98 and 284-285; MySQL emits 'SELECT ' for scalar functions (line 153) and only refuses the table-valued case. SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315). Pinned by call.test.ts lines 105/112/119/135/142/165.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallKind.Procedure._why: default-call.ts emits a procedure as its own statement per dialect: emitPostgresCall/emitMysqlCall prepend 'CALL ' (lines 93, 171) and emitMssqlCall prepends 'EXEC ' (line 278); the top-level SQLite guard (lines 310-315) throws before any kind branch. Pinned by call.test.ts lines 19/38/49/63.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallParamDirection.In._why: An In argument is bound positionally on every proc-supporting dialect: emitPostgresArgs/emitMysqlArgs/emitMssql*Args fall through to addDynamicValue(param.value) (lines 80, 136, 243/264); Postgres also allows named form 'name := value' (line 70) and MSSQL '@name = value' (line 240). MySQL only rejects a *named* In (line 118), so positional In is native. SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315).

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallParamDirection.InOut._why: InOut is emitted per engine in default-call.ts: Postgres binds the input value like In (falls to the else at line 80); MySQL seeds the session variable with a prefixed 'SET @name = value; ' then passes '@name' (lines 162-169, 124-134); MSSQL DECLAREs the local with '= value' and passes '@name = @name OUTPUT' (lines 202-234). SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315); InOut is refused inside functions on every dialect (lines 317-326). Pinned by call.test.ts lines 283/321.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallParamDirection.Out._why: default-call.ts realizes Out differently per engine: Postgres passes the slot as NULL since it has no variables (lines 75-78); MySQL emits a session variable '@name' (lines 124-134); MSSQL DECLAREs a typed local and passes it as '@name = @name OUTPUT' (lines 184-234). Name (and sqlType on MSSQL) are required preconditions, not capability gaps. SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315); Out is also refused inside functions on every dialect (lines 317-326). Pinned by call.test.ts lines 264/298/333.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallReturnIntent.ResultSet._why: A set-returning/table-valued function wraps the call in 'SELECT * FROM ': Postgres line 95 and MSSQL line 285. MySQL has no table-valued functions and throws verbatim at line 150. SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315). Pinned by call.test.ts lines 112/126/142.

mysql.refusal: MySQL does not support table-valued functions

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallReturnIntent.Scalar._why: A scalar function return wraps the call in plain SELECT: Postgres line 97 ('SELECT '), MySQL line 153 ('SELECT '), MSSQL line 285 (the non-ResultSet branch, 'SELECT '). SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315). Pinned by call.test.ts lines 105/119/135.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

CallReturnIntent.Void._why: Void is the procedure return intent; the procedure branches in emitPostgresCall/emitMysqlCall/emitMssqlCall (lines 91-102, 171-174, 277-281) ignore returnIntent and emit CALL/EXEC with no SELECT wrapper, so Void parses natively on all three proc-supporting engines. SQLite refuses all calls (lines 310-315). (callFunction separately rejects Void in the builder, but that is a function-only guard, not a parser refusal of this member.)

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support stored procedures or functions (CALL/EXEC)

FrameBoundType.CurrentRow._why: emitFrameBound (default-window.ts:21-22) emits the constant 'CURRENT ROW' for all four dialects with no branching or throw. Allowed on MSSQL even within RANGE (mssql_range_frame.test.ts → 'RANGE CURRENT ROW').

FrameBoundType.Following._why: emitFrameBound (default-window.ts:24-25) emits '${offset} FOLLOWING' (e.g. '1 FOLLOWING') for every dialect with no dialect branch; window.test.ts renders 'ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING'. As with Preceding, the only MSSQL numeric-offset refusal comes from combining it with RANGE (default-window.ts:114-124, adjudicated under FrameUnit.Range), not from this bound type — MSSQL emits 'N FOLLOWING' in a ROWS frame. Native on all four.

FrameBoundType.Preceding._why: emitFrameBound (default-window.ts:18-19) emits '${offset} PRECEDING' (e.g. '5 PRECEDING') for every dialect with no dialect branch. On MSSQL it emits fine inside a ROWS frame (mssql_range_frame.test.ts:82-90 → 'ROWS 5 PRECEDING'); the only MSSQL refusal for a numeric offset is when it is combined with RANGE, which is captured under FrameUnit.Range (default-window.ts:114-124), not attributable to this bound type itself. Native on all four.

FrameBoundType.UnboundedFollowing._why: emitFrameBound (default-window.ts:27-28) emits the constant 'UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING' for all four dialects with no branching and no throw. Confirmed by window.test.ts (SQLite → 'RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING').

FrameBoundType.UnboundedPreceding._why: emitFrameBound (default-window.ts:15-17) emits the constant 'UNBOUNDED PRECEDING' for all four dialects with no branching and no throw. Exercised on Postgres/SQLite/MSSQL in window.test.ts and mssql_range_frame.test.ts (e.g. 'RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING' allowed on MSSQL).

FrameUnit.Range._why: default-window.ts:126 emits 'RANGE ' for every dialect; the one branch (lines 114-124) throws on MSSQL only when RANGE is paired with a numeric-offset bound, which is a constraint on the argument, not on the unit — MSSQL genuinely HAS the RANGE unit (mssql_range_frame.test.ts allows 'RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING' and 'RANGE CURRENT ROW'). Stays consistent with the existing all-native decisions.json adjudication for FrameUnit.Range; Postgres/MySQL/SQLite emit RANGE with offsets freely (mssql_range_frame.test.ts:100-117).

FrameUnit.Rows._why: default-window.ts:126 emits the literal 'ROWS ' for FrameUnit.Rows with no dialect branching, and the sole throw path (lines 114-124) is gated on FrameUnit.Range, so ROWS never refuses on any engine. Confirmed by window.test.ts (ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING, ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) and mssql_range_frame.test.ts:82-90 (MSSQL emits ROWS 5 PRECEDING).

FullTextMode.Boolean._why: default-full-text.ts emits a native boolean full-text predicate on all four engines. Postgres routes to to_tsquery( (postgresTsQueryFunction, lines 32-34, inside to_tsvector(...) @@ ...); MySQL emits MATCH (...) AGAINST (...) closed with ' IN BOOLEAN MODE)' (lines 97-105, 173-176); MSSQL falls through to CONTAINS( (lines 135-138); SQLite treats Boolean the same as Natural and emits '<col> MATCH ' (the guard on line 149 throws only when the mode is NEITHER Natural NOR Boolean, so Boolean passes, lines 153-156). Pinned by tier3.test.ts (IN BOOLEAN MODE) and full_text_phrase.test.ts (CONTAINS( for MSSQL Boolean; to_tsquery for Postgres).

FullTextMode.Natural._why: default-full-text.ts emits a native natural-language full-text predicate on all four engines. Postgres routes to plainto_tsquery( (postgresTsQueryFunction default branch, lines 31-39, wrapped in to_tsvector(...) @@ ..., lines 52-80); MySQL emits MATCH (...) AGAINST (...) closed with ' IN NATURAL LANGUAGE MODE)' (lines 97-105, 173-180); MSSQL emits FREETEXT( (lines 128-133); SQLite emits '<col> MATCH ' (lines 149-156). Pinned by tier3.test.ts (plainto_tsquery; "d"."body" MATCH hello) and full_text_phrase.test.ts (FREETEXT( for MSSQL Natural, IN NATURAL LANGUAGE MODE for MySQL).

mysql.name: MATCH … AGAINST … IN NATURAL LANGUAGE MODE

FullTextMode.Phrase._why: Completes the partial adjudication in decisions.json (enums.FullTextMode.Phrase), matching its refusal wording verbatim. Only Postgres supports a phrase mode: default-full-text.ts routes it to phraseto_tsquery( (lines 35-37), which joins terms with the <-> distance operator (a true phrase match) — tier3/full_text_phrase tests assert phraseto_tsquery and not plainto_tsquery. MySQL and MSSQL throw because a phrase is expressed by quoting the bound search value, not by a mode (lines 89-95 and 119-125); SQLite throws because FTS only supports Natural/Boolean MATCH (line 150). All three throws pinned in full_text_phrase.test.ts. (decisions.json names the Postgres capability 'phrase'; the raw SQL term the parser actually emits is phraseto_tsquery.)

mssql.refusal: MSSQL expresses a phrase by quoting it inside the CONTAINS search condition, not by a mode — pass a quoted phrase to a Boolean search, or use whereMatchRaw

mysql.refusal: MySQL expresses a phrase by quoting the search string, not by a mode — pass a quoted phrase to a Boolean search, or use whereMatchRaw

sqlite.refusal: SQLite FTS only supports Natural/Boolean-style MATCH queries

HintKind.ForceIndex._why: default-hint.ts:mysqlIndexHintForTable emits `FORCE INDEX (idx)` only for Mysql (line 43); non-MySQL dialects are refused first by validateHints (lines 100-107 via to-sql.ts:127). tier3.test.ts:242-245 pins MySQL `FORCE INDEX`, and 295-300 pins the non-MySQL DELETE refusal. Same MySQL-only gate as UseIndex.

mssql.refusal: MySQL index hints (hintUseIndex/hintForceIndex) are only supported on MySQL — use hintRaw elsewhere

postgres.refusal: MySQL index hints (hintUseIndex/hintForceIndex) are only supported on MySQL — use hintRaw elsewhere

sqlite.refusal: MySQL index hints (hintUseIndex/hintForceIndex) are only supported on MySQL — use hintRaw elsewhere

HintKind.MssqlOption._why: default-hint.ts:emitTrailingHints (lines 62-79, called at to-sql.ts:295) emits ` OPTION (<text>)` only when config.databaseType===Mssql; every other dialect throws the line 65-68 message. tier3.test.ts:259-266 pins the MSSQL `OPTION (RECOMPILE)` emission.

mysql.refusal: hintMssqlOption is only supported on MSSQL — use hintRaw on other dialects

postgres.refusal: hintMssqlOption is only supported on MSSQL — use hintRaw on other dialects

sqlite.refusal: hintMssqlOption is only supported on MSSQL — use hintRaw on other dialects

HintKind.Raw._why: default-hint.ts:emitTrailingHints (lines 81-88, called at to-sql.ts:295) has NO dialect gate for the Raw branch — it only rejects empty text ('hintRaw requires non-empty SQL') and otherwise appends the caller's raw string verbatim. Neither validateHints nor mysqlIndexHintForTable touches Raw, so it is accepted on all four dialects as a caller-owned passthrough.

HintKind.UseIndex._why: default-hint.ts:mysqlIndexHintForTable emits `USE INDEX (idx)` only when config.databaseType===Mysql (lines 33-46); for every other dialect validateHints (lines 100-107, invoked unconditionally at to-sql.ts:127 before the query-type dispatch) throws first. tier3.test.ts:236-239 pins the MySQL `USE INDEX` emission and 286-292 pins the non-MySQL refusal.

mssql.refusal: MySQL index hints (hintUseIndex/hintForceIndex) are only supported on MySQL — use hintRaw elsewhere

postgres.refusal: MySQL index hints (hintUseIndex/hintForceIndex) are only supported on MySQL — use hintRaw elsewhere

sqlite.refusal: MySQL index hints (hintUseIndex/hintForceIndex) are only supported on MySQL — use hintRaw elsewhere

JoinOperator.Equals._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 309-311 for column-to-column, 353-355 for column-to-value) emits '=' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.GreaterThan._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 315-317, 359-361) emits '>' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.GreaterThanOrEquals._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 318-320, 362-364) emits '>=' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.LessThan._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 321-323, 365-367) emits '<' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.LessThanOrEquals._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 324-326, 368-370) emits '<=' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.Like._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 327-329, 371-373) emits 'LIKE' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no JoinOperator specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

JoinOperator.NotEquals._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 312-314, 356-358) emits '<>' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinOperator.NotLike._why: default-join.ts ON-clause switch (lines 330-332, 374-376) emits 'NOT LIKE' with no dialect branch, native on every engine.

JoinType.Cross._why: default-join.ts line 64-66 emits 'CROSS JOIN ' unconditionally for every dialect, no throw.

JoinType.CrossApply._why: default-join.ts line 79-91: MSSQL emits 'CROSS APPLY ', Postgres/MySQL emit 'CROSS JOIN LATERAL ', SQLite throws (line 91). tests/shared/outer_apply_on_clause.test.ts lines 66-80 pin CROSS JOIN LATERAL (PG/MySQL) and CROSS APPLY (MSSQL).

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support CROSS APPLY/LATERAL joins

JoinType.FullOuter._why: default-join.ts line 58-63: only MySQL throws; MSSQL/Postgres/SQLite fall through to emit 'FULL OUTER JOIN '. tests/mysql/join.test.ts line 95 pins the MySQL throw; tests/postgres/join.test.ts line 97 pins the FULL OUTER JOIN emit.

mysql.refusal: MySQL does not support FULL OUTER JOIN

JoinType.Inner._why: default-join.ts line 43-45 emits 'INNER JOIN ' unconditionally, no dialect branch. tests/{mysql,postgres,sqlite}/join.test.ts pin identical INNER JOIN output across dialects.

JoinType.Lateral._why: default-join.ts line 67-78: SQLite throws (line 69), MSSQL throws directing to APPLY methods (line 71-76), and Postgres/MySQL fall through to emit 'JOIN LATERAL ' (line 77). tests/shared/tier3.test.ts and outer_apply_on_clause.test.ts corroborate LATERAL support on PG/MySQL and refusal on SQLite.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL LATERAL joins use CROSS APPLY/OUTER APPLY — use joinCrossApply/joinOuterApply

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support LATERAL joins

JoinType.Left._why: default-join.ts line 46-48 emits 'LEFT JOIN ' unconditionally for every dialect, no throw.

JoinType.LeftOuter._why: default-join.ts line 49-51 emits 'LEFT OUTER JOIN ' unconditionally for every dialect, no throw.

JoinType.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no JoinType specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

JoinType.OuterApply._why: default-join.ts line 92-104: MSSQL emits 'OUTER APPLY ', Postgres/MySQL emit 'LEFT JOIN LATERAL ', SQLite throws (line 104). tests/shared/outer_apply_on_clause.test.ts lines 26-48 pin LEFT JOIN LATERAL (PG/MySQL), OUTER APPLY (MSSQL), and the SQLite refusal.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support OUTER APPLY/LATERAL joins

JoinType.Right._why: default-join.ts line 52-54 emits 'RIGHT JOIN ' unconditionally — MySQL is NOT excluded (over-attribution trap avoided). tests/mysql/join.test.ts line 53-65 pins the MySQL RIGHT JOIN output.

JoinType.RightOuter._why: default-join.ts line 55-57 emits 'RIGHT OUTER JOIN ' unconditionally, including MySQL. tests/{mysql,postgres,sqlite}/join.test.ts pin RIGHT OUTER JOIN output for each.

JsonExtractMode.Object._why: In ts/query/src/parser/default-json.ts emitJsonExtractExpression, Object mode (the non-Text branch) emits Postgres '->' (line 36), MySQL JSON_EXTRACT(col, path) without the JSON_UNQUOTE wrap (lines 51-55), MSSQL JSON_QUERY(col, path) (lines 60-66), and for SQLite it THROWS (lines 78-82) because json_extract always returns text. Object mode is not exercised in the corpus (only Text is), but the emitter branches are unambiguous and default-json-predicate.ts reuses this same function for the where/having variants.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite json_extract always returns text — use JsonExtractMode.Text or selectJsonRaw

JsonExtractMode.Text._why: In ts/query/src/parser/default-json.ts emitJsonExtractExpression, Text mode emits distinct native terms per dialect: Postgres '->>' (line 36), MySQL wraps as JSON_UNQUOTE(JSON_EXTRACT(col, path)) (lines 43-47), MSSQL JSON_VALUE(col, path) (lines 69-73), SQLite json_extract(col, path) (lines 85-89). Pinned by tests/shared/tier3.test.ts (Postgres ->>' , MSSQL JSON_VALUE) and contract/corpora/emission/corpus.json around line 21740-21906 (all four dialects).

NullsOrder.First._why: default-order-by.ts line 44 sets hasNativeNulls only for Postgres and SQLite; line 64 then emits ' NULLS FIRST' for those two, while lines 46-52 throw the ParserError for MySQL and MSSQL. Completing the existing decisions.json entry: native SQL term is NULLS FIRST; the two refusals are the verbatim interpolated throw (dialectDisplayName yields 'MySQL'/'MSSQL').

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no NULLS FIRST/LAST — order by a nullability expression explicitly if you need it

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no NULLS FIRST/LAST — order by a nullability expression explicitly if you need it

NullsOrder.Last._why: Same code path as NullsOrder.First: default-order-by.ts line 64 emits ' NULLS LAST' when hasNativeNulls (Postgres/SQLite, line 44), and lines 46-52 throw for MySQL and MSSQL. Native SQL term is NULLS LAST; the MySQL/MSSQL refusals are the verbatim throw and match the pinned decisions.json strings.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no NULLS FIRST/LAST — order by a nullability expression explicitly if you need it

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no NULLS FIRST/LAST — order by a nullability expression explicitly if you need it

NullsOrder.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no NullsOrder specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

OrderByDirection.Ascending._why: default-order-by.ts emitOrderByTerm lines 56-57 append the literal ' ASC' with no dialect branch whatsoever, so every engine gets the same term. Native on all four; the SQL term emitted is ASC.

OrderByDirection.Descending._why: default-order-by.ts emitOrderByTerm lines 58-59 append the literal ' DESC' with no dialect branch, identical across engines. Native on all four; the SQL term emitted is DESC.

OrderByDirection.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no OrderByDirection specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

RowLockMode.ForShare._why: default-row-lock.ts:33 emits the trailing 'FOR SHARE' clause for Postgres/MySQL (row_lock.test.ts asserts `... FOR SHARE;`). MSSQL throws in mssqlRowLockHint (lines 92-96) because T-SQL has no shared-mode counterpart to UPDLOCK — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE. SQLite throws at lines 22-25.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no shared row lock — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE; use forUpdate() or take the isolation level explicitly

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

RowLockMode.ForUpdate._why: default-row-lock.ts:33 emits the trailing 'FOR UPDATE' clause for Postgres/MySQL (pinned in row_lock.test.ts). MSSQL takes the mssqlRowLockHint path which returns ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK)' (line 99/112, pinned in mssql_row_lock_coverage.test.ts). SQLite hits the guard at lines 22-25 and throws.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

RowLockMode.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no RowLockMode specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

RowLockWait.Default._why: The default block-until-available wait behavior. In default-row-lock.ts neither the trailing-clause path nor mssqlRowLockHint appends any suffix for Default (it falls through to the plain 'FOR UPDATE'/'WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK)'), so it emits nothing and never throws on its own. Treated as a sentinel: native '(none)' on all four dialects.

RowLockWait.Nowait._why: default-row-lock.ts:35-36 appends ' NOWAIT' for Postgres/MySQL (row_lock.test.ts asserts `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT`). MSSQL does NOT throw — mssqlRowLockHint line 101-103 emits ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK, NOWAIT)', so NOWAIT is native there (the 'MSSQL refuses' hypothesis is over-attribution; there is no wait-mode throw in source). SQLite throws first at lines 22-25 before any wait suffix.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

RowLockWait.SkipLocked._why: default-row-lock.ts:37-38 appends ' SKIP LOCKED' for Postgres/MySQL (row_lock.test.ts). MSSQL does NOT throw — mssqlRowLockHint line 108-109 emits ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK, READPAST)', READPAST being Microsoft's own term for the skip-locked behavior (mssql_row_lock_coverage.test.ts asserts READPAST). SQLite throws at lines 22-25.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

UpsertAction.DoNothing._why: Per ts/query/src/parser/default-upsert.ts and default-insert.ts: MySQL DoNothing takes the isMysqlInsertIgnore() path — defaultInsert emits an 'INSERT IGNORE' prefix (default-insert.ts:59-61) and emitUpsertClause emits nothing (default-upsert.ts:92-95); pinned by tests/shared/upsert.test.ts:50 ('INSERT IGNORE INTO ...'). Postgres/SQLite fall through to the shared branch emitting ' ON CONFLICT' + optional (cols) + ' DO NOTHING' (default-upsert.ts:103-113; upsert.test.ts:14,37). MSSQL is refused up front in defaultInsert (lines 45-51) whenever state.upsertState is present, before emitUpsertClause runs — MERGE is a separate statement/enum, not this INSERT-upsert path (test upsert.test.ts:80 asserts /MSSQL has no upsert/).

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

UpsertAction.DoUpdate._why: Per ts/query/src/parser/default-upsert.ts: MySQL DoUpdate emits ' ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE ' + SET list (default-upsert.ts:97-99; tests/shared/upsert.test.ts:109). Postgres/SQLite emit ' ON CONFLICT' + (conflict cols, required here) + ' DO UPDATE SET ' + SET list (default-upsert.ts:103-120; upsert.test.ts:95). MSSQL is refused in defaultInsert (lines 45-51) whenever an upsertState is present — the emitUpsertClause MSSQL guard (default-upsert.ts:72-77) is unreachable because defaultInsert throws first; MERGE is a separate native T-SQL surface, not this path (test tier3.test.ts:82 asserts /MSSQL has no upsert/).

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

UpsertAction.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no UpsertAction specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

WhereOperator.Contains._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 177-209 wrap the value as '%value%' with LIKE metachars escaped and emit '<col> LIKE ? ESCAPE <escape-literal>' on all four dialects (MSSQL additionally escapes '['; MySQL doubles the ESCAPE literal). Confirmed by tests/shared/contains.test.ts. No dialect throws.

WhereOperator.EndsWith._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 177-209 build pattern '%value' (suffix) and emit '<col> LIKE ? ESCAPE ...' on all four dialects. No dialect throws.

WhereOperator.Equals._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case Equals emits '=' on every dialect (line 216); default-where.ts routes it via emitComparisonPredicate (lines 171-178). Null values are collapsed to 'IS NULL' (lines 27-34) but the operator term is '='. Never dialect-gated.

WhereOperator.GreaterThan._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case GreaterThan emits '>' on every dialect (line 222), no dialect-gating.

WhereOperator.GreaterThanOrEquals._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case GreaterThanOrEquals emits '>=' on every dialect (line 225), no dialect-gating.

WhereOperator.Ilike._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 101-124: only Postgres emits ' ILIKE ' (line 114); MySQL, MSSQL and SQLite fall through to the throw at line 119-123. tests/shared/ilike.test.ts pins '"u"."email" ILIKE $1' on Postgres and the throw on the other three. Refusal text verbatim from source (dialectDisplayName + concatenated string).

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no ILIKE operator — its LIKE case-sensitivity is collation-dependent

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no ILIKE operator — its LIKE case-sensitivity is collation-dependent

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no ILIKE operator — its LIKE case-sensitivity is collation-dependent

WhereOperator.Iregex._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 126-175 with insensitive=true: Postgres emits case-insensitive ' ~* ' (line 140); MySQL emits REGEXP_LIKE(col, ?, 'i') (lines 157-166); SQLite/MSSQL throw (line 171-174). tests/shared/regex.test.ts pins REGEXP_LIKE(`u`.`email`, ?, 'i').

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no built-in regular-expression operator

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no built-in regular-expression operator

WhereOperator.IsDistinctFrom._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 37-99: Postgres and SQLite emit native ' IS DISTINCT FROM ' (lines 44-52); MySQL emits the rewrite 'NOT (col <=> value)' using its native null-safe operator (lines 64-69); MSSQL has no native operator but emits a sound bound-value-aware rewrite '(col <> value OR col IS NULL)' (or 'col IS NOT NULL' for a NULL value) at lines 75-98 — no dialect throws. tests/shared/is_distinct_from.test.ts pins all four.

WhereOperator.IsNotDistinctFrom._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 37-99: Postgres and SQLite emit native ' IS NOT DISTINCT FROM ' (lines 44-52); MySQL emits its genuinely native null-safe equality operator '<=>' (lines 57-62); MSSQL has no native operator but emits the sound bound-value-aware rewrite 'col = value' (or 'col IS NULL' for a NULL value) at lines 75-90 — no dialect throws. tests/shared/is_distinct_from.test.ts confirms MySQL '<=>' and the MSSQL rewrites.

WhereOperator.LessThan._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case LessThan emits '<' on every dialect (line 228), no dialect-gating.

WhereOperator.LessThanOrEquals._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case LessThanOrEquals emits '<=' on every dialect (line 231), no dialect-gating.

WhereOperator.Like._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case Like emits 'LIKE' on every dialect (line 234); the caller supplies the wildcards. No dialect branch throws.

WhereOperator.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no WhereOperator specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

WhereOperator.NotContains._why: Same branch as Contains (comparison-operator.ts lines 177-209); NotContains is the only one that emits ' NOT LIKE ' (line 204), pattern '%value%' with ESCAPE, on all four dialects. tests/shared/contains.test.ts pins '... NOT LIKE $1 ESCAPE'. No dialect throws.

WhereOperator.NotEquals._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case NotEquals emits '<>' on every dialect (line 219). A null value collapses to 'IS NOT NULL' (lines 27-34) but the operator term is '<>'. No dialect branch throws.

WhereOperator.NotIlike._why: Same branch as Ilike (comparison-operator.ts lines 101-124); the negate flag makes Postgres emit ' NOT ILIKE ' (line 114). MySQL/MSSQL/SQLite hit the same throw (line 119-123). tests/shared/ilike.test.ts pins '"u"."email" NOT ILIKE $1' on Postgres.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no ILIKE operator — its LIKE case-sensitivity is collation-dependent

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no ILIKE operator — its LIKE case-sensitivity is collation-dependent

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no ILIKE operator — its LIKE case-sensitivity is collation-dependent

WhereOperator.NotIregex._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 126-175 with negate=true and insensitive=true: Postgres emits ' !~* ' (line 140); MySQL emits NOT REGEXP_LIKE(col, ?, 'i') (lines 158-166); SQLite/MSSQL throw (line 171-174). tests/shared/regex.test.ts pins "NOT REGEXP_LIKE(`u`.`email`, ?, 'i')".

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no built-in regular-expression operator

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no built-in regular-expression operator

WhereOperator.NotLike._why: comparison-operator.ts switch case NotLike emits 'NOT LIKE' on every dialect (line 237). No dialect branch throws.

WhereOperator.NotRegex._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 126-175 with negate=true: Postgres emits ' !~ ' (line 140); MySQL prefixes 'NOT ' then REGEXP_LIKE(col, ?, 'c') → NOT REGEXP_LIKE (lines 158-166); SQLite/MSSQL throw (line 171-174). tests/shared/regex.test.ts pins "NOT REGEXP_LIKE(`u`.`email`, ?, 'c')".

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no built-in regular-expression operator

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no built-in regular-expression operator

WhereOperator.Regex._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 126-175: Postgres emits case-sensitive ' ~ ' (line 140); MySQL emits REGEXP_LIKE(col, ?, 'c') (lines 157-166); SQLite and MSSQL throw at line 171-174. tests/shared/regex.test.ts pins REGEXP_LIKE(`u`.`email`, ?, 'c') and both refusals.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no built-in regular-expression operator

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no built-in regular-expression operator

WhereOperator.StartsWith._why: comparison-operator.ts lines 177-209 build pattern 'value%' (prefix) and emit '<col> LIKE ? ESCAPE ...' on all four dialects. No dialect throws.
